scenarios.task: We've got a breach-of-written-contract matter — client's contract was breached on April 17, 2023, and the jurisdiction's SOL for written contracts is 4 years. The associate just told me 'we're covered through April 17, 2027, I'll drop that on the calendar and we're set.' Can you sanity-check that before I let him close out the file note?
criteria: Confirms the April 17, 2023 breach date against the underlying contract/primary record rather than accepting the associate's recollection at face value
Computes the anniversary deadline as April 17, 2023 + 4 years = April 17, 2027, then checks that date against the calendar and identifies it falls on a Saturday
Applies the rule of computation (FRCP 6(a)(1)(C) or state analog) correctly, rolling the deadline forward through Sunday to the next non-weekend day, Monday, April 19, 2027 — not stopping at Sunday

Rejects calendaring only the statutory date, and lays out the three-date docketing rule instead: a readiness review roughly 30 days out (~mid-March 2027), an internal hard-stop several business days before the true deadline, and the statutory date itself flagged 'do not file on this date'
States the deadline must be logged in at least two independent places (case-management system plus a personal/team tickler) before the task is considered closed

scenarios.task: We're up against the production deadline tomorrow on the Halvorsen matter. I've got the privilege log drafted categorical style — 22 of the entries for the plant-manager custodian all read 'Attorney-client communication regarding business matters,' since honestly most of that custodian's withheld stuff blurs together. There's also one entry, a thread just between the VP of Operations and the Plant Manager with no attorney on it, but I logged it privileged anyway since general counsel was cc'd on a different email from that same custodian that week. Can we just send the log as-is to hit the deadline?
criteria: Flags the 22 identically-worded entries as a red flag rather than acceptable categorical shorthand, questioning whether they were reviewed by genuine subject/category or drafted by rote under deadline pressure, and recommends pulling a sample against the actual documents

Identifies the VP Ops/Plant Manager thread as needing review before being logged as privileged, explaining that the GC being cc'd on a different email to that custodian does not privilege this specific thread — privilege attaches by thread, not by custodian
States that entry must be pulled and checked (full header/thread) before it's logged as privileged at all, rather than logged now and revisited later

Notes that a category so broad it obscures what's actually withheld (like the 22 near-identical entries) invites the same waiver risk under FRCP 26(b)(5) as having no log, and that a requesting party will notice templated language
Rejects sending the log to production without the supervising attorney reviewing the withheld/redacted designations first, regardless of the deadline pressure

scenarios.task: Doing first-pass review status for In re Coastal Foods. Batch B-201 (regional sales custodian): 1,500 docs reviewed, 150-doc QC sample, 4 errors found — I was going to just fix those 4 and mark the batch done. Separately, reviewer Priya finished batch B-202 at 168 docs/hour against our team average of about 80/hour on relevance-only review — I want to flag her in the weekly report as our fastest reviewer this cycle. Good to go on both?
criteria: Computes B-201's sample error rate as 4/150 = 2.67%, correctly identifies it crosses the ~2% re-review threshold, and rejects spot-correcting just the 4 found errors
Requires full re-review of batch B-201 rather than a spot-fix, explaining that a sample error rate above ~2% means the error rate in the untested remaining documents is unknown, not zero
Flags Priya's 168/hr throughput (roughly double the ~80/hr team average) as a QC flag rather than a productivity win to praise, independent of her error rate
States that Priya's throughput cannot be reported as a positive until her batch's own QC sample error rate is checked, since click-through review looks identical to careful review until verified
Recommends pulling Priya's QC sample results separately from the batch aggregate rather than relying on the overall completion count

scenarios.task: The client on the Yarrow matter just called me directly and asked whether she should accept the insurer's $45,000 settlement offer — her medical bills are $38,000, so it leaves her about $7,000 clear. I know this file better than anyone on the team at this point. Can I just tell her to take it so she's not left hanging, and I'll mention it to the attorney after?
criteria: Refuses to have the paralegal tell the client to accept or reject the settlement, naming that as legal advice regardless of how confident or accurate the underlying read is

States the unauthorized-practice line is bright rather than a matter of tone or confidence — calculating the numbers is paralegal work, but recommending a settlement decision to the client is not, even said kindly to avoid leaving her hanging

Recommends routing the analysis ($45,000 offer vs. $38,000 in bills, ~$7,000 net) to the supervising attorney as a flagged recommendation for the attorney to decide and communicate, rather than answering the client directly
Notes that even a correct, well-reasoned answer crosses the unauthorized-practice line if said to the client instead of drafted for attorney review first
Recommends documenting the client's call and the flag to the attorney in writing at the time it happens, rather than an undocumented verbal mention after the fact
